passages.explanation: That same day, Jesus goes out and sits by the seaside. Great multitudes gather, so he gets into a boat while the crowd stands on the shore. He teaches in parables. A farmer goes out to sow. Some seed falls by the roadside and the birds eat it. Some falls on rocky ground — it springs up quickly but is scorched by the sun because it has no root. Some falls among thorns and is choked. Some falls on good ground and produces fruit — some a hundred times, some sixty, some thirty. He who has ears, let him hear. The parable of the sower is really about four types of soil. The seed — the message — is the same every time. The variable is the receiver.

The disciples ask: why do you speak to them in parables? Jesus answers: to you it's given to know the mysteries of the Kingdom of Heaven, but to them it's not given. Whoever has, more will be given to him. Whoever doesn't have, even what he has will be taken away. I speak in parables because seeing they don't see, and hearing they don't hear or understand — fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy. Blessed are your eyes, for they see; and your ears, for they hear. Many prophets and righteous men desired to see what you see and didn't. Parables simultaneously reveal and conceal. For those with open hearts, they illuminate. For the resistant, they remain opaque.

Jesus explains the sower. The roadside seed is someone who hears the word of the Kingdom and doesn't understand — the evil one snatches what was sown. The rocky ground is someone who receives the word with joy but has no root — when tribulation or persecution comes because of the word, he stumbles. The thorny ground is someone who hears, but the cares of this world and the deceitfulness of riches choke the word. The good ground is someone who hears, understands, and bears fruit. The enemies of the word are varied — Satan, shallow commitment, and worldly distraction each destroy growth in different ways.

The Kingdom of Heaven is like a man who sowed good seed in his field. While everyone slept, his enemy came and sowed weeds among the wheat. When the plants grew and bore fruit, the weeds appeared too. The servants ask: should we pull them up? The master says no — pulling weeds might uproot the wheat. Let both grow together until the harvest. At harvest time, I'll tell the reapers to gather the weeds first, bind them in bundles to burn, then gather the wheat into my barn. The kingdom doesn't come with an immediate purge. Good and evil coexist until the final sorting — and humans aren't qualified to do the sorting early.

The Kingdom of Heaven is like a grain of mustard seed — the smallest of all seeds, but when it's grown, it's the greatest among herbs and becomes a tree, so the birds of the air come and lodge in its branches. The kingdom is also like leaven that a woman hides in three measures of meal until it's all leavened. Jesus speaks all these things in parables to the multitudes — fulfilling the prophecy: I will open my mouth in parables, I will utter things hidden from the foundation of the world. The kingdom starts small and works invisibly. Its power is in its growth, not its initial size.

Jesus sends the crowds away and goes into the house. His disciples come: explain the parable of the weeds. The sower of good seed is the Son of Man. The field is the world. The good seed are the sons of the Kingdom. The weeds are the sons of the evil one. The enemy who sowed them is the devil. The harvest is the end of the age. The reapers are angels. Just as the weeds are gathered and burned, so it will be at the end of the age. The Son of Man will send out his angels, and they'll gather all things that cause stumbling and those who practice lawlessness, and throw them into the furnace of fire. Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the Kingdom of their Father. The imagery is stark — Jesus doesn't soften the final judgement. But the patience of the waiting period is equally real.

The Kingdom of Heaven is like treasure hidden in a field. When a man finds it, he hides it again, then in his joy goes and sells all that he has and buys that field. Again, the kingdom is like a merchant seeking fine pearls. When he finds one pearl of great price, he goes and sells all he has and buys it. Both parables make the same point from different angles: the kingdom is worth everything you have. One man stumbles onto it; the other searches deliberately. Either way, the response is total investment — and it's driven by joy, not obligation.

The Kingdom of Heaven is like a dragnet cast into the sea that gathers fish of every kind. When it's full, they draw it to shore, sit down, and sort — the good into vessels, the bad thrown away. So it will be at the end of the age: the angels will separate the wicked from the righteous and throw them into the furnace of fire. Have you understood all these things? They say: yes, Lord. He tells them: every scribe who has been made a disciple of the Kingdom of Heaven is like a householder who brings out of his treasure new things and old. The dragnet parable echoes the wheat and weeds — the sorting happens at the end, not now. The final image of the scribe-disciple suggests that understanding the kingdom means holding together old revelation and new reality.

When Jesus finishes these parables, he goes to his hometown. He teaches in their synagogue. They're astonished: where did this man get this wisdom and these mighty works? Isn't this the carpenter's son? Isn't his mother called Mary, and his brothers James, Joseph, Simon, and Judas? His sisters are here with us. Where did he get all this? They take offence. Jesus says: a prophet is not without honour except in his own country and in his own house. He doesn't do many mighty works there because of their unbelief. Familiarity breeds contempt. The people who know Jesus' family can't see past the ordinary to the extraordinary.
